summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should 'choose boring technology culture' by focusing on organizational health rather than cultural innovation. She distinguishes between formal organizational culture (which should be stable and load-bearing) and informal culture (which emerges naturally from people when the formal structure is healthy). The core thesis is that business success earns you innovation tokens, and most companies should spend those tokens sparingly on culture, instead focusing on executing well.
key_points: Culture should serve the business, not exist for its own sake — business success earns innovation tokens to spend on cultural experiments
Distinguish between formal organizational culture (structural, load-bearing) and informal culture (emergent, bottoms-up, fun)
Organizational health — minimal politics, minimal confusion, high morale, high productivity — trumps cultural innovation
Leaders should build stable formal culture that creates safety for informal culture to thrive naturally
Success and achievement are the real fuel for workplace joy and bonding, not manufactured fun
money_quotes: If culture means everything, then culture means nothing.

You want formal culture to be boring, stable, reliable, load-bearing…because this creates a safe structure for people to bring the humor, the fun, the joy, the delight, without any fear of building collapse.
An organization that is healthy will inevitably get smarter over time.
You don't gather at work for the fun of it, you come together to do great things.
People are fucking weirdos. If you create an emotionally safe zone and the conditions for success, informal culture will thrive.
key_insight: Great workplace culture isn't something leaders manufacture — it emerges naturally when they focus on organizational health and business success, creating the stable foundation that frees people to bring their own joy and creativity.
